content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
February 22,
1995
Morgan Foley,
Esq.
McCormick, Kabot, Michner
& Foley
1220 West Main Street
Visalia, California
93201
Re:
Your Request For Informal
Assistance
Our File No.
1—95—043
Dear Mr.
Foley:
We respond to your request for written advice on behalf of
Porterville City Councilmember Daryl C. Nicholson regarding the
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the
“Act”).
1
Your request does not contain sufficient information to
allow us to respond with formal written advice.
2
Accordingly,
we
accept your request as one for informal assistance.
Informal
assistance does not provide the requestor with immunity from
possible Commission enforcement action,
nor is it evidence of good
faith in any other proceeding.
(Regulation 18329(c) (3).)
QUESTION
Does Councilmember Nicholson have a conflict of interest with
respect to a city council decision regarding a general plan
amendment and zoning changes for a parcel of land which will be
purchased by a competitor of a business owned by Councilmember
Nicholson?
CONCLUSION
Councilmember Nicholson will have a conflict of interest in
each of the decisions if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections
1,8000—18954.
2
In a February 8,
1995,
telephone conversation,
you indicated
that Councilmember Nicholson has chosen not to supplement the
facts regarding foreseeability and materiality due to the
difficulty of acquiring such information.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807
•
(916) 322-5660


File No.
1—95—043
Page
2
decisions will have a material effect on the councilmember’s
business.
The decisions will have a material effect on the
councilmember’s business if either or both of the decisions result
in:
(1)
an increase or decrease in gross revenues for a fiscal
year of $10,000.00 or more; or
(2) the business incurring or
avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing
expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $2,500.00 or more; or
(3) the business increasing or decreasing its assets or
liabilities by $10,000.00 or more.
FACTS
Daryl C. Nicholson is a councilmember of the City of
Porterville.
The city council is considering a general plan
amendment changing the designation and zoning for a specific site
from professional and office to heavy commercial.
The subject
property is currently in escrow.
The purchaser is a business
located on an adjacent parcel which leases equipment and operates
a hardware store.
Councilmember Nicholson is a 75% owner of a
competing equipment rental business in the City of Porterville.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official from
making, participating in making,
or otherwise attempting to
influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason
to know that he has a financial interest.
A public official has a
financial interest in a governmental decision within the meaning
of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision
will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from the
decision’s effect on the public generally,
on,
among other things,
any business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect
investment interest worth $1,000.00 or more.
(Section 87103.)
Reasonable foreseeability is a question of fact.
An effect
is reasonably foreseeable when it is substantially likely to
occur.
A mere possibility is not considered reasonably
foreseeable; however, absolute certainty is not required.
(In re
Thorner
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
198.)
Logic dictates that a decision
involving a competitor’s business may have some possible indirect
effects on the councilmember’s interests.
However,
you have given
no information which would indicate whether or not the city
council decisions are substantially likely to affect Councilmember
Nicholson’s business interests.
The Commission does not operate
as a finder of fact in providing written advice.
(In re Oglesbv
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
71.)
Accordingly,
we are unable to state
whether or not indirect effects on the councilmember’s business
are reasonably foreseeable.
If an effect on the councilmember’s business is reasonably
foreseeable, it would become necessary to analyze whether or not
the reasonably foreseeable effect was material.
Materiality is
determined by reference to Commission regulations.
Regulation
18702.2 provides specific guidance on whether a reasonably


File No.
1—95—043
Page
3
foreseeable effect is material to a business entity indirectly
affected by a governmental decision.
We assume that the councilmember’s business, like most small
businesses, is covered by 18702.2(g).
Regulation 18702.2(g)
provides that an effect is material on a business indirectly
affected by a governmental decision if the decision will result
in:
(1)
an increase or decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal
year of $10,000.00 or more; or,
(2) the business incurring or
avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing
expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $2,500.00 or more; or
(3)
an increase or decrease in the value of. the business assets of
$10,000.00 or more.
If any of the above three conditions are met,
the effect is material and the councilmember would be disqualified
from participating in the decision.
3
We trust that this brief analysis is helpful to the
councilmember as he undertakes his public duties.
This office
remains available to provide further advice should additional
facts.
If you have any questions,
you may contact the undersigned
at
(916)
322—5901.
Sincerely,
Steven C.
Churchwell
By:
Daniel E.
Muallem
Counsel,
Legal Division
We assume that the effects, if any,
on the councilmember’s
business would be unique and therefore distinguishable from the
effect on the public generally. ( Regulation 18703.)

(3) the business increasing or decreasing its assets or
